responses: Earwax (cerumen) is a natural substance that protects the ear canal. Impaction occurs when this wax builds up enough to cause symptoms like hearing loss or discomfort.
Earwax is actually a sign of a healthy ear; it traps dust and bacteria. It only becomes a problem (impaction) when the ear's natural self-cleaning process is interrupted.

patterns: Why is my ear blocked with wax?
responses: The most common cause of impaction is the use of cotton swabs (Q-tips) or other objects to clean the ear, which actually pushes the wax deeper into the canal.
Using hearing aids, earplugs, or earbuds frequently can also block the natural outward flow of wax. Some people simply produce more or drier wax than others due to genetics.

patterns: What are symptoms of ear wax impaction?
responses: Common signs include a feeling of fullness in the ear, muffled hearing, earache, and 'tinnitus' (ringing in the ear).
In some cases, impacted wax can cause a chronic cough (due to nerve stimulation) or even dizziness (vertigo) if the wax is pressing against the eardrum.

patterns: How to prevent ear wax buildup?
How to keep ears clean safely?
responses: The best prevention is to leave your ears alone. The 'nothing smaller than your elbow' rule applies—your ears are self-cleaning and usually don't need help.
If you are prone to buildup, you can use a few drops of mineral oil or baby oil once a week to keep the wax soft so it can migrate out naturally.
There is no direct medical link between specific foods and earwax production. However, staying hydrated helps keep all bodily secretions, including earwax, from becoming too dry and hard.

Some anecdotal evidence suggests that diets high in Omega-3 fatty acids and Vitamin C support overall skin health within the ear canal, but dietary changes are rarely a primary treatment for impaction.

patterns: Can I use peroxide in my ear?
responses: Over-the-counter softening drops (like Debrox) containing carbamide peroxide can help break up hard wax. Alternatively, plain mineral oil or hydrogen peroxide diluted with water can be used.
WARNING: Do not use drops if you suspect you have a perforated (holed) eardrum or an ear infection. If drops don't work, see a doctor for professional irrigation or manual removal.
